questions.question_text: What is the output of the following code? for (var i = 0; i < 3; i++) { setTimeout(() => console.log(i), 100); }
questions.explanation: The output is '3 3 3' because `var` is function-scoped, and the `setTimeout` callbacks execute after the loop has completed, sharing the same `i` reference which is now 3. Using `let` (block-scoped) or an IIFE would capture the correct value per iteration.

questions.question_text: What is logged to the console? console.log(1); setTimeout(() => console.log(2), 0); Promise.resolve().then(() => console.log(3)); console.log(4);
questions.explanation: The order is '1 4 3 2'. Synchronous code (1, 4) runs first. Promise callbacks (microtasks) execute before the next macrotask (setTimeout), so 3 logs before 2. This demonstrates the event loop's prioritization of microtasks over timers.

questions.question_text: Consider: const obj = { name: 'Test', log() { console.log(this.name); } }; const log = obj.log; log(); What is the output?
questions.explanation: The output is `undefined` because `log()` is called as a standalone function, so `this` is either the global object (non-strict mode) or `undefined` (strict mode). It no longer references `obj`. Using `obj.log()` or an arrow function (which captures `this` lexically) would log 'Test'.

questions.question_text: What happens when an async function throws an error? async function test() { throw new Error('fail'); } test().catch(console.error);
questions.explanation: An async function always returns a promise. Throwing inside it rejects that promise, so the returned promise's `catch` method handles the rejection. This is analogous to `return Promise.reject(error)`; the error propagates to the caller's promise chain.

choices.choice_text: The error is caught by the `catch` method
The error must be caught inside `test` with `try/catch`

questions.question_text: Given: function Person() {} Person.prototype.sayHi = function() { console.log('Hi'); }; const p = new Person(); p.sayHi(); What if we add `Person.prototype.sayBye = ...` after `p` is created?
questions.explanation: Instances delegate to the prototype object, which is mutable. Adding `sayBye` to `Person.prototype` after `p` is created means `p` will still find `sayBye` via its internal `[[Prototype]]` link. The prototype chain is looked up at call time, not at instance creation time.

choices.choice_text: `p.sayBye()` will work because `p`'s prototype references the same object
`p.sayBye()` is undefined; prototypes are frozen at instantiation
Adding to prototype after instantiation throws an error
`p` must be recreated with `new Person()` to see `sayBye`
